questions.questionText: The most superficial layer of the epidermis is:
questions.optionB: Stratum corneum (outermost layer — composed of dead, anucleated, keratin-filled corneocytes forming the skin's primary barrier)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Epidermis layers bottom to top: basale → spinosum → granulosum → lucidum (palms/soles only) → corneum. Why correct: Stratum corneum = outermost, composed of 15-20 layers of dead, flat, anucleated corneocytes. Lipid-rich intercellular matrix forms 'brick-and-mortar' structure (barrier function). Why A wrong: Basale (germinativum) = deepest, contains stem cells and melanocytes. Why C wrong: Spinosum = 'prickle cell layer,' desmosomes (intercellular bridges), Langerhans cells. Why D wrong: Granulosum = keratohyalin granules (profilaggrin), lamellar bodies release lipids. Clinical relevance: Pemphigus vulgaris: suprabasal split (above basale). Bullous pemphigoid: subepidermal (below basale). Acantholysis = loss of intercellular connections within epidermis. Exam trap: Stratum lucidum is found ONLY in thick skin (palms, soles). Memory aid: 'Epidermis = Basically, Spines Get Loaded with Cornified layers (Basale-Spinosum-Granulosum-Lucidum-Corneum).' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Basic Dermatology.
questions.conceptTag: Epidermis layers stratum corneum barrier basale spinosum
